posts.title: Spec-first front-end planning
posts.summary: A practical way to turn a fuzzy homepage idea into sections, states, and tests before anyone opens CSS.
posts.contentHtml:
A spec does not need to be heavy to be useful. The point is to name the audience, the job of the page, the content model, and the failure states before the layout starts accumulating clever exceptions.

For a small static site, I like to write the routes first, then sketch the repeated components, then list the exact checks that should pass before publishing. That gives the design a useful amount of friction.

The payoff is calmer implementation. Every card, link, preview, and generated page has a reason to exist, and visual polish can happen without losing the structure underneath it.

posts.title: Designing a content model for small teams
posts.summary: How a small shared model keeps posts, work samples, photography, and link notes from becoming separate little kingdoms.
posts.contentHtml:
The easiest content model to maintain is the one editors can describe without a diagram. A post has a title, summary, date, tags, image, and destination. Everything else is a view of that stream.

That constraint keeps the homepage, blog, and work index honest. The same piece can be promoted, filtered, or archived without copying it into another special format.

Small teams usually need fewer fields than they think. They need the fields to be named clearly and used consistently.

posts.title: CareBridge Companion
posts.summary: A SwiftUI companion app concept for member IDs, receipts, plan details, and benefit questions.
posts.contentHtml:
A mobile companion app concept for health plan members who need receipts, member IDs, plan details, and benefit questions in one calm place.

The prototype uses large task cards, plain labels, and short conversational prompts so the app feels useful before it feels comprehensive.

posts.title: Neighborhood Giving Map
posts.summary: Interactive community impact map for Clearfork Beverage Co.
posts.contentHtml: An interactive map that turns regional impact data into a browsable story, with place-based markers, live metrics, and a focused call to explore local detail.

posts.title: Financial Wellness Library
posts.summary: A 52-post financial literacy library covering everyday planning, household trade-offs, and money decisions.
posts.contentHtml: A structured financial wellness library built from practical, plain-language articles on budgeting, saving, credit, and household planning.

posts.title: Writing useful summaries for busy readers
posts.summary: Notes on making previews direct enough to help someone decide what to read next.
posts.contentHtml:
A summary is not a teaser. It is a small act of service. The reader should know what the piece covers, why it matters, and whether it is worth their next few minutes.

The strongest summaries use concrete nouns, plain verbs, and one clear promise. They do not need to reveal every argument, but they should not hide the subject behind mood.

When the preview does its job, the full article starts with a reader who already understands the contract.

posts.title: A calm launch checklist
posts.summary: A practical pass through content, polish, and quality before a site goes live.
posts.contentHtml:
The best launch checklist is boring in the right ways. Read every page out loud, click every primary path, check generated pages, and make sure the empty states still sound like the same person.

Then look at the site in the browser sizes people actually use. The last mile is usually spacing, image framing, touch targets, and stray placeholder copy.

posts.title: Making placeholder content do real work
posts.summary: How draft content can test structure, tone, and editorial workflow before launch.
posts.contentHtml:
Placeholder content is useful when it behaves like the real thing. A good draft title wraps awkwardly, a summary has a point of view, and a sample image has the same visual weight as the final asset.

That kind of placeholder helps reveal whether a design can hold actual editorial work. It also makes review easier because people can react to rhythm, density, and hierarchy instead of guessing through gray boxes.
